edges.description: Severe PAH genotypes converge on complete or near-complete enzyme deficiency.
Residual-function PAH variants create milder phenotypes and possible BH4 response.
edges.target: Residual PAH Activity and BH4 Responsiveness

edges.description: Complete or near-complete PAH activity loss is the severe end of hepatic PAH enzyme deficiency.
Residual-function alleles still reduce hepatic PAH activity, but less completely than classic PKU genotypes.
edges.source: Residual PAH Activity and BH4 Responsiveness

edges.description: Reduced PAH activity impairs phenylalanine clearance from blood.
Blocked conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine reduces tyrosine availability.
Elevated blood phenylalanine is the direct biochemical readout of impaired PAH-dependent phenylalanine clearance.
Hyperphenylalaninemia contributes to an elevated phenylalanine-to-tyrosine ratio.
Excess phenylalanine competitively perturbs transport of other neutral amino acids into brain.
edges.target: Competitive Large Neutral Amino Acid Transport at the Blood-Brain Barrier

edges.description: Persistent systemic hyperphenylalaninemia increases brain phenylalanine burden and toxicity.
Excess phenylalanine is diverted to alternative metabolites, including phenylketones.
Chronic hyperphenylalaninemia requires phenylalanine-restricted medical nutrition; inadequate protein status during therapy can impair linear growth.
PKU and its long-term phenylalanine-restricted medical nutrition context are associated with lower bone mineral density in some cohorts.
edges.target: Reduced Bone Mineral Density in PKU

edges.description: Elevated maternal phenylalanine during early gestation creates a fetal teratogenic exposure associated with congenital heart defects.
Protein insufficiency during PKU dietary therapy can manifest as growth delay or linear growth impairment.
Plasma prealbumin is a monitoring readout for protein sufficiency during phenylalanine-restricted therapy.
Reduced bone mineral density is the quantitative substrate for osteopenia in the PKU phenotype spectrum.
edges.source: Reduced Bone Mineral Density in PKU

edges.description: Bone mineral density Z-score is a quantitative readout of reduced bone density in PKU cohorts.
edges.source: Reduced Bone Mineral Density in PKU

edges.description: Maternal PKU teratogenicity can cause congenital heart defects in offspring.
Reduced PAH-mediated conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine is reflected by decreased blood tyrosine.
Reduced tyrosine availability contributes to the elevated phenylalanine-to-tyrosine ratio.
Reduced tyrosine supply limits melanin production.
Lower tyrosine availability reduces substrate supply for catecholamine synthesis.
Lower brain tryptophan availability contributes to impaired serotonin synthesis.
edges.source: Competitive Large Neutral Amino Acid Transport at the Blood-Brain Barrier

edges.description: Lower brain tyrosine availability contributes to impaired dopamine synthesis.
edges.source: Competitive Large Neutral Amino Acid Transport at the Blood-Brain Barrier

edges.description: Monoamine deficits contribute to attention, executive, and behavioral impairment.
Dopaminergic dysfunction contributes to executive and cognitive deficits.
Biogenic amine disruption in classical PKU can contribute to hypertonicity and spasticity.
Brain phenylalanine toxicity contributes to persistent white-matter and subcortical abnormalities.
edges.target: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

edges.description: Untreated high-phenylalanine neurotoxicity can impair brain growth and cause acquired microcephaly.
Brain dysfunction manifests clinically as cognitive impairment and executive deficits.
Severe phenylalanine-mediated brain dysfunction can manifest as encephalopathy.
Phenylalanine-mediated brain dysfunction can be reflected by abnormal EEG findings.
PKU brain dysfunction can include tremor as a motor manifestation.
PKU neurologic involvement can include ataxia.
Structural brain injury is associated with lower cognitive performance.
edges.source: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

edges.description: Persistent white-matter injury is the mechanistic counterpart of the HPO white-matter morphology phenotype.
edges.source: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

edges.description: White-matter and subcortical motor-pathway injury can contribute to lower-limb spasticity.
edges.source: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

edges.description: Cerebral visual impairment is represented as an occasional neurologic manifestation downstream of brain structural injury.
edges.source: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

edges.description: Severe or untreated neurotoxicity can progress to intellectual disability.
Severe untreated neurotoxicity can present with epilepsy/seizures.
Untreated or poorly controlled PKU neurotoxicity can manifest as global developmental delay.
Persistent cognitive and executive dysfunction can manifest as specific learning disability.
intermediate_mechanisms: reduced global intelligence and executive function.

edges.description: Adult PKU neurocognitive effects include attention and executive-function impairment.
PKU brain dysfunction can present with behavioral problems.
Neuropsychiatric effects of PKU include anxiety in the phenotype spectrum.
Neuropsychiatric effects of PKU include depression in the phenotype spectrum.
Severe chronic neurocognitive deterioration can manifest as dementia in some PKU patients.
Reduced melanin production leads to fair skin and lighter pigmentation phenotypes.
Phenylketone byproducts contribute to characteristic musty body odor.
Eczematous dermatitis is associated with untreated or poorly controlled PKU through incompletely defined skin effects of PAH deficiency.
Urinary phenylalanine and phenylketone excretion reflects overflow alternative phenylalanine metabolism.
Phenylpyruvic acid is a measured urinary readout of phenylketone accumulation.

edges.target: Residual PAH Activity and BH4 Responsiveness
Competitive Large Neutral Amino Acid Transport at the Blood-Brain Barrier
Competitive Large Neutral Amino Acid Transport at the Blood-Brain Barrier

edges.description: Elevated blood phenylalanine reports impaired PAH-dependent phenylalanine clearance.
Elevated maternal blood phenylalanine during early gestation predicts fetal congenital-heart-defect risk in maternal PKU syndrome.

Reductions in plasma phenylalanine during treatment with phenylalanine hydroxylase activators (sapropterin) or phenylalanine-metabolizing enzyme therapy (pegvaliase) report pharmacodynamic correction at the hyperphenylalaninemia node and underpin the FDA surrogate-endpoint basis for traditional approval of PKU drugs.
Lower blood tyrosine reports reduced PAH-mediated conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine.
Elevated Phe/Tyr ratio reports combined phenylalanine accumulation and reduced tyrosine production.
Elevated Phe/Tyr ratio reports the biochemical imbalance produced by impaired PAH flux.
Elevated urinary phenylpyruvic acid reports diversion of excess phenylalanine into phenylketone metabolites.
Lower plasma prealbumin reports protein insufficiency associated with impaired linear growth in treated PKU children.
Decreased BMD Z-score reports reduced bone mineral density in PKU bone-health assessment.
edges.source: Reduced Bone Mineral Density in PKU

evidence.reference_title: Genetic etiology and clinical challenges of phenylketonuria.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, traditionally known as phenylketonuria, results in the accumulation of phenylalanine in the blood of affected individuals and was the first inborn error of metabolism to be identified through population screening.

evidence.reference_title: Nutrient intake and congenital heart defects in maternal phenylketonuria.
evidence.snippet: A significantly increased incidence of congenital heart defects was observed in offspring of mothers with hyperphenylalaninemia who had an elevated blood phenylalanine level >10 mg/dL at 0 to 8 weeks of gestation and a protein intake of < or = 50% of the recommended dietary allowance (P <.0013).

evidence.snippet: Pharmacological treatments are available, such as tetrahydrobiopterin, which is effective in only a minority of patients (usually those with milder PKU)

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of sapropterin dihydrochloride (tetrahydrobiopterin, 6R-BH4) for reduction of phenylalanine concentration in patients with phenylketonuria: a phase III randomised placebo-controlled study.
evidence.snippet: In some patients with phenylketonuria who are responsive to BH4, sapropterin treatment to reduce blood phenylalanine could be used as an adjunct to a restrictive low-phenylalanine diet, and might even replace the diet in some instances.

evidence.reference_title: The complete European guidelines on phenylketonuria: diagnosis and treatment.
evidence.snippet: If left untreated, PKU results in increased phenylalanine concentrations in blood and brain, which cause severe intellectual disability, epilepsy and behavioural problems.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylketonuria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001250 | Seizure | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Biallelic severe PAH variant combinations produce complete or near-complete PAH activity loss and map to the classic PKU mutation-to-phenotype path.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Classic phenylketonuria is caused by a complete or near-complete deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase activity and without dietary restriction of phenylalanine most children will develop profound and irreversible intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic etiology and clinical challenges of phenylketonuria.
evidence.snippet: Severe phenotypes are classic PKU, and less severe forms of PAH deficiency are moderate PKU, mild PKU, mild hyperphenylalaninaemia (HPA), or benign HPA.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylketonuria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000736 | Short attention span | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Phenylketonuria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001328 | Specific learning disability | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of sapropterin dihydrochloride (tetrahydrobiopterin, 6R-BH4) for reduction of phenylalanine concentration in patients with phenylketonuria: a phase III randomised placebo-controlled study.
evidence.snippet: We aimed to test the efficacy of sapropterin, a synthetic form of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), for reduction of blood phenylalanine concentration.

evidence.reference_title: Pegvaliase for the treatment of phenylketonuria: Results of a long-term phase 3 clinical trial program (PRISM).
evidence.snippet: Pegvaliase, PEGylated recombinant Anabaena variabilis phenylalanine ammonia lyase (PAL), converts Phe to trans-cinnamic acid and ammonia, and is a potential enzyme substitution therapy to lower blood Phe in adults with PKU.

readouts.interpretation: Reductions in plasma phenylalanine during treatment with phenylalanine hydroxylase activators (sapropterin) or phenylalanine-metabolizing enzyme therapy (pegvaliase) report pharmacodynamic correction at the hyperphenylalaninemia node and underpin the FDA surrogate-endpoint basis for traditional approval of PKU drugs.

evidence.reference_title: Phenylketonuria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001337 | Tremor | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Treated adults still exhibit white-matter damage and subcortical volume loss associated with phenylalanine burden.
nodes.id: White Matter and Subcortical Structural Injury

evidence.reference_title: Compromised white matter is related to lower cognitive performance in adults with phenylketonuria.
evidence.snippet: In conclusion, our findings demonstrate that white matter alterations in early-treated phenylketonuria persist into adulthood, are most prominent in the posterior white matter and are likely to be driven by axonal damage.

evidence.reference_title: Volumetric brain reductions in adult patients with phenylketonuria and their relationship with blood phenylalanine levels.
evidence.snippet: Moreover, these patients showed reduced global white matter volume as well as reductions in the volume of several subcortical grey matter structures, which might be related to the existence of underlying neurodevelopmental alterations.
